message:
> Why do we ask certain citizens to pay back welfare and not others?

Because some aren't citizens.

By most estimates, illegal immigrants result in annual contributions of [$13 billion to social security](https://www.theatlantic.com/business/archive/2016/09/undocumented-immigrants-and-taxes/499604/), while extracting only $1 billion. Illegal immigrants are actually a net positive for social security revenues, by a significant margin.

It's estimated that roughly half of those eligible to pay federal income taxes, do so. Illegal immigrants also pay into local sales and property taxes along with state taxes, generating an additional $10.6 billion a year.

By federal law, much like any other children, undocumented children are also guaranteed free public schooling. Additionally, if they *are* US citizens, then children are entitled *as full and complete US citizens* to welfare benefits.

Moreover, federal law dictates that undocumented citizens are *ineligible* for welfare like Medicaid, Supplemental Security Income, SNAP, TANF grants, or HUD assistance. If they are receiving welfare on behalf of their children, that is their entitled right as US citizens to receive that benefit.

As the welfare was for the benefit directly of a US citizen, even if an undocumented parent happens to benefit it makes little to no sense to try to force someone to pay back something they are full within legal right to ask for. Why do we ask certain citizens to pay back welfare and not others?

message: Sex =/= relationship. There is nothing inherently sexual about LGBTQ relationships, discussing two boys falling in love or getting married is no more sexual than telling kids about princes marrying princesses or hearing your teacher say she's married to a man.

There's a difference between the portrayal of LGBT relationships/identities and discussing those relationships. For example, a lot of parents don't want their children to be exposed to information about sex AT ALL, which excludes both heterosexual and LGBT relationships. 

EDIT: to clarify here, I don't personally hold this viewpoint, but I am very familiar with it (raised in a conservative religious community). For a lot of relationship types you indeed can use child friendly terms (a gay couple are two men who love each other, babies come from two parents who want to have a child, etc.) but this isn't always the case with some sexual identities, such as asexuals.

The lend lease program  gave away $667 billion in supplies to the world. message:
>Germany and Italy were not expecting to fight the Commonwealth and France in 1939. Hitler's focus was on the conquest and subjugation of Eastern Europe with the eventual invasion of the USSR. He thought that any Western war would happen years later. The Western powers declaring war on Germany was a surprise to Hitler and diverted much of Germany's forces away from the Eastern Front. Hitler had to delay his invasion of the Soviet Union to ready his army for the Eastern Front after battles in the Western Front. 

I doubt Hitler invading in 1940 vs 1941 would have made a serious difference, even taking the Western war out of the equation. First, the Wehrmacht in 1940 was barely ready to take on France and Britain. The Fall of France was as much due to Allied incompetence and luck as it was due to German brilliance. In 1940, the vast majority of German tanks were obsolete light tanks, namely the Panzer I and II. While the more advanced Panzer III and IVs were available, at the time they had only been produced in small numbers and were only about a quarter of the Panzer corps. These tanks were no better than if not inferior to the BT and T-26 light tanks that made up the build of Soviet armor (not to mention the T-34s and KV-1s), which the Soviets had far more of. Second, invading France first actually helped Germany in a lot of ways for Barbarossa. It removed a powerful enemy in the West and allowed him to concentrate more forces to the East. The Fall of France also provided Germany with huge amounts of much needed equipment, not to mention additional industrial capacity. At Dunkirk, the Wehrmacht seized 2,472 guns, 20,000 motorcycles, and almost 65,000 other vehicles including 445 tanks. This was just from the BEF. Without this additional materiel, it is unlikely that a 1940 invasion of the Soviet Union would have fared much better. If anything, the delay bought Germany time to become better prepared to fight the Red army. 

>Besides their losses in ground forces and usage of troops to occupy Western countries,

If France hasn't been knocked out at this time Germany would still have to station significant forces in the West to guard against the possibility of a French attack.

>Germany spent a massive amount of resources to construct Western fortifications against a naval invasion,

construction of the Atlantic Wall didn't really start til 1942 and didn't get seriously ramped up until Rommel arrived in early 1944. By that time the tides had already turned on the Eastern Front. In any case much of those resources was in the form of things like concrete. The Eastern Front was largely a mobile war; building lots of bunkers and pillboxes ultimately would have made little difference.

>suffered millions of casualties against non-USSR allied forces in the Western Front, Italy, Africa, 

Most of these didn't occur until 1943-44, again after the Eastern Front was already more or less decided. 

>and in their air force and navy

The Eastern Front was largely a land conflict. Air losses mattered but not naval ones. 

>Also, the lend-lease to the Soviet Union would have been far smaller or non-existent if Western countries and the US had not declared war.

Most Lend Lease didn't arrive until mid-1943, after the Battle of Kursk. By that point it was clear Germany would lose. Lend Lease helped but it wasn't decisive 

I think you're thinking about this too much from a tactical perspective  and ignoring the strategic aspects that accounted for Germany's victory. WWII was ultimately decided by factors off the battlefield. These factors would have been pretty much unchanged if the USSR fought Germany alone 

First, natural resources. Germany simply didn't have enough raw materials to sustain its war effort while the Soviet Union did. Oil was of particular importance, the only oil available to Germany was a few wells in Romania while the Soviets had the Caucasus and Siberia. A large part of why Hitler was so dead set on invading the Soviet Union was to seize the natural resources himself. In fact, invading in 1940 would have made the commodity situation worse. In 1940 the Germans and Soviets signed a trade agreement where the Soviets supplied them with critical ore, rubber, grain and oil in exchange for machinery and technology. This kept the war machine running until 1941. Without this agreement Germany would have been unable to overrun France, much less Eastern Europe. Invading in 1940 obviously prevent this trade from happening, proving a fatal case of biting the hand that feeds you. Lack of grain and rubber stores in particular would have made an invasion of the USSR in 1940 impossible.

Second, population. The Soviet Union had more than twice the population that Germany (190 million vs 80 million). This is why the Red Army could keep fighting even after sustaining massive casualties. Even without the Western Allies, the Wehrmacht would be facing an opponent twice as large as itself. 

Third, industry. The Soviets produced more of everything over the course of the war. This was partially due to the fact that the Soviets had greater industrial capacity to begin with (even in 1940 the Soviets were ahead in production in pretty much all areas except naval armament). Part of it was since the Soviets totally mobilized much quicker. It was also due to the fact that German manufacturing was not particularly efficient; the Germans made too many overly complex models while the Soviets stuck to a few simple designs. This wouldn't have changed without the Western powers. Also, without Western Europe and its industry under Hitler's boot, the disparity between Germany and the USSR would have been greater.

Finally, geography. This is probably the most crucial factor. The Soviet Union was huge and that sheer size made invading it an extreme challenge. The farther the Germans advanced the more they overstretched their supply lines, problems compounded by the Wehrmacht's poor logistical system (logistics have never been the German's strong suit) and the USSR"s poor infrastructure (the rails in the Soviet Union were of a different gauge than those in Germany). Given the huge amount of strategic depth, the Soviets could afford to trade space for time and not worry about losing anything too important. And then there's General Winter to take into account. Even if the Germans could focus more of their resources to the Eastern front, they would have struggled to move it across the vast distances of the Steppe. 

Ultimately, the Western Allies made a significant contribution but I think the Soviets could have won without them. It would have been a much costlier victory, but a victory nonetheless.

message:
I would ask you to please elaborate on what you consider gynocentric about modern society? Do you find that the workplace caters more to women than to men? Do you believe that husbands typically work harder than their wives, physically and emotionally?
  
As a man, I find that pretty much everything in society is working in my favor. I do not have to endure sexist comments at work. Any housework I actually do is considered praiseworthy rather than expected. My hobbies and interests are not considered "childish" or "silly" even when they totally are. I have never been called bitchy, vain, or emotional for being honest about my feelings. I never worry about being harassed or sexually assaulted if I go to a party or a bar. I've never been catcalled. I know with confidence that my weight or physical appearance will never affect my job prospects. 
  
I struggle to think of any way in which my life would be easier if I was a woman. I suppose I would be less likely to be drafted or less responsible for "protecting my home" if there was an intruder. That's all I got.

No it does not undermine my point, because the area that is cleared of this crime is suddenly super cool and brings in a bunch of commerce for the city, and the surrounding areas may have to shoulder some increased crime, but no one area is shouldering all of it. It is dispersed across a vast region. So yeah the crime rate in several different neighborhoods in Chicago might rise from 10% to 12%, but another area is now completely safe to walk in at night and brings in a bunch of tax money from businesses

Suppose an area has two social groups, A and B. A are poorer, and have a higher crime rate (say 3.9%, by some measure) than B (say, 0.1%)

Currently, they live intermingled in some area, that therefore has a crime rate of 2%. 

Now, you allow gentrification to happen. Members of A are priced out, and leave. The crime rate drops to 0.1%.

Except it doesn't drop, of course. Members of A still live somewhere, and that area has seen the crime rate rise from 2% to 3.9%. The overall crime rate is still 2%. 

Perhaps more, in fact, since now you have racial tension between those "snooty" B and those "terrible" A.

If you want to reduce the crime rate, you can't do it by getting rid of people and then selectively ignoring them.

message:
Your view seems to be completely unfalsifiable. Yes, Brady has been fortunate to have a great team and organization around him - he's not the only QB to spend his career in an advantageous situation, but his is certainly one of if not the best.

So what? The only argument you've made is that the Patriots have done well during limited stretches with lesser QBs. Well, okay. Brady has no control over that, so it seems absurd to judge him by it. Like, would the Patriots have done poorly if Peyton Manning was their QB in the same circumstances? Would that somehow make Manning better? It doesn't make any sense.

Of course an otherwise excellent team can still have some success with a subpar QB. That proves the Patriots have been excellent, which nobody ever disputed.

It doesn't prove anything about Brady. As you seem to acknowledge, there's no significant measure, in terms of success, accolades, or statistics, by which Brady does not rank among the very best of all-time. There are literally *six* other QBs who have won at least *half* as many games as him.

What could he possibly have done better to convince you he's one of the best?

But you're not doing that in any sort of nuanced way, and the sample size is tiny. You're saying: A won with this team, and B won with this team, so A and B are about the same, probably.

That's not justified. What if the pattern is: this team is good, so it mostly wins as long as X is above a certain threshold of adequacy. In that case, X=A where A is the GOAT and X=B where B is all right will have very similar results. That seems a lot more plausible than Tom Brady just being the luckiest player in NFL history by a country mile.

Edit: I'd also add that even if it were a valid point, it chucks out every other piece of evidence available, almost all of which puts Brady near the top. If you're going to argue a view contrary to the vast majority of expert opinion, you should probably address that evidence as well. "He had a good team" doesn't explain away almost two decades of excellent results.

message:
As opposed to what? It wasn't selfish to bring a child into the world when children were just free labor on the family farm, and acknowledged as such? Now is the only time in human history a child costs their parents a buttload of money (I think it was something like $200,000 to bring a child up to the age of 18 and that doesn't include college), and we are going to say NOW they are being selfish? By the way, families can still have only one child and they are still bending the population curve of the world down. 

Secondly, having a child inspires me to make the world a better place for them after I die. I'm not the only one. I think you should include the calculus of how children make us better than how they make the world worse. 

Either way, as a biomass humans are still dwarfed by insects, for example. What matters is not how many children we have but what choices they make for the sake of the world.

"Oh wow, @dense-tea, so having kids is the new green movement? I guess I'll start a family to save the planet. Maybe my kids can start a recycling business. And @savage-arrangement, $200,000? message:
Well it *is* racist to judge a person by their race and be more or less fearful of that person based on that. That's just what the word means. What I think you're really trying to say is that it isn't always a terrible thing to do. The connotation of the word racist shouldn't be how this is evaluated, so let's detach the word "racist" from the word "bad" for just a minute.

We judge situations by how the feel intuitively sometimes. That means taking mental shortcuts, including assumptions or associations based on race, gender, accent, the clothes someone is wearing -- basically anything you can observe.

We live in societies with racial tension. It varies from country to country and city to city, but it's usually, if not always there in some form. White and black people who do not know each other are less likely to approach each other and start talking on the street. If a person approaches another person on the street of another race then it might be more likely that there is some malicious intent than if it were two people of the same race. 

If you're white and a black man approaching you might make you apprehensive, that *is* racist. It just isn't other things that are so commonly treated as synonymous with racist. It isn't aggressive. It isn't immoral. It isn't irrational.

But it is still racist and using that word is important for two reasons. You must admit to yourself that you are, just like everyone else, at least a little bit racist. Realizing you're racist is necessary so you can regulate your racism so it does not become something that is aggressive, irrational, or immoral. It is easy to cross that line without realizing it because sometimes the same instincts which are sensible as they are common -- whether right or wrong -- will lead you to be a racist jerk. 

Those gut reactions may be all you have to go on sometimes, and you shouldn't be so politically correct that you put yourself in a dangerous situation. But that same gut reaction should yield to good judgment and compassion. For example, if your friend introduces you to a friend of his who is black and has tattoos or whatever else might make you not ask him for directions on the street, put that shit aside, say hello, shake his hand, and treat him with the same respect as anyone else. (I'm sure I don't need to tell you this. I'm just trying to make the point completely, not be condescending.) On the same token, a teacher should always give every student the same fair chance, a salesman should give every potential customer the same amount of respect, etc...

**TLDR: It is racist, but it's not evil.** You're fine if you keep racist thoughts and instincts in check, treat people with respect always, and give everyone a fair chance when you meet them. The ethical thing to do is not to try not to be racist, but instead realize that you *are* racist and just try not to be an asshole. We're all racist. The people we commonly call racists are the ones who do not regulate their thoughts and actions but instead allow those biases to run rampant and pervasively influence how they regard others.

Let's all just take a deep breath and remember that we're all just trying to navigate this crazy world. And maybe, just maybe, we can do it without labeling every little thing. Cheers!"

If an individual clearly isn't in control of the truly random decisions, movements, thoughts that they perform, how can you say that their will is free?

Free will most certainly exists. 

Imagine we mastered time travel. Now we can set up an experiment to definitely prove the existence or non existence of free will. 

Put Subject in a room with 52 cards face down on the table, ask the subject to turn one face up at random. 

Record the result, go back in time and repeat the experiment. 

BEFORE you jump to the conclusion that going back in time would yield the same result.  Consider this.  

We are probably pretty far from mastering time travel.  But we are closer to effectively wiping out someone's memory.  If/when we could master that would could replicate the experiment to a very high degree. Everything would have to be controlled.  The room, the temperature, the oxygen levels, any stimulus you can think could be controlled. 

Now you wipe the Subjects memory.  Put them in a room with 52 cards face down, ask them to turn one over at random.  Record the result. 

Wipe their memory again, put them in the room ask them to flip a card over at random. 

You no longer intuitively believe the same card will be flipped over! In fact your 98% certain the subject will chose a different card. 

The only thing letting someone believe the same card would be chosen, in the first scenerio, is the introduction of a sci-fi fantasy of a 'time line' and time travel.  

In the second scenerio you are forced to take that away and look at the basic facts of what is happening you will see that there are millions of little  decisions, movements, thoughts that we each perform every day that are truly random, and the only word for random is free-will when it comes to human decisions.

"Oh wow, @merry-frequency, you're like a philosopher with a deck of cards! But seriously, if we're talking about free will and time travel, why not just ask a superhero? They seem to handle these paradoxes better than us mere mortals. And @coral-dive, your memory wipe experiment sounds like a plot from a sci-fi movie. Maybe we should just stick to flipping coins instead of cards. It's less complicated and no one gets their memory wiped. 😂"

"Sure, @coral-dive, let's just wipe people's memories and play card games. Because that's totally ethical and not at all like a dystopian novel. Maybe we should just ask the cards if they have free will. I bet they'd say, \"We're just pieces of cardboard, but thanks for the existential crisis!\" \ud83d\ude02"

this is the "you too" fallacy, your argument is invalid.

Not really, for three reasons:

1) I'm not actually arguing that it's okay because of what OP does that's similar.  I'm pointing to OP's own experience and asking them to question whether their implicit assumptions about lawful conduct are reasonable.  If OP can look at their own experience and see how unreasonable it is to view the law as they do, the argument works.

2) I'm demonstrating that OP's position is inconsistent with their own behavior in the current moment, not just the past.  They can still adhere to the law and choose to adhere to it in the future and their position and actions will be perfectly consistent.  If not, they will be a hypocrite.  That doesn't mean the idea is wrong in and of itself, but it's often hard to consciously hold two contradictory ideas to be true simultaneously.

The subreddit is called ChangeMyView, not ProveMeWrong.

3) I'm demonstrating the practical consequences of following the implicit assumptions that OP makes in their own life.  If the law should be as respected as OP says as a matter of principle, OP should modify their behavior in certain ways.  If they don't wish to do that, they would again be a hypocrite.

message: A BSU is not an inherently political organization.  A BSU is not nominally dedicated to a symbol of genocide.  Rules against inherently political groups and groups nominally dedicated to a symbol of genocide completely fair game in my view.

As long as rules are applied consistently I don't really have a maximum bound.  In this case, if a University can attract a diverse group of students that feel safe on campus with a law abiding, but incendiary, Swastika Appreciation Society, I'm good with it.  In the infinitely more likely case that they can't, the University could prohibit all supremacist groups, and groups that engage in supremacist behavior, if that wasn't enough, they could ban all groups that engage in discussion on politically incendiary topics. 

If a behavior is tolerated for Black people, it should be tolerated for White people.  I don't think that's very controversial.

message:
>The problems I'm referring to are the increasing inequality of wealth and power, the ineffectiveness of the political system, and the immeasurable impact of wealth on that political system.

You aren't going to get money out of politics.  Ever.  It's a pipe dream; you cannot have a $15 trillion economy and a government that doesn't pay attention to those who make all that money.  Bearing that in mind, demonizing "money" in politics is simplistic.  The campaign contribution of a common citizen is money just like a larger contribution from a PAC, and one problem with those common citizens is that they're doing a shit job of organizing themselves and producing coherent political goals.

Example: the Women's March after the election drew millions of people who paid a great deal of money for busses, accommodations, food, signs, pink hats...and some folks caused major property damage.  What *didn't* they do?  Give money to Elizabeth Warren's campaign.  [She needs it.](http://dailycaller.com/2017/01/23/new-poll-shows-tough-re-election-road-for-sen-warren/)  So okay, you made your feelings known...but your nominal leader might lose her seat in two years.  Is that the system failing, or you?

The same could be said of Black Lives Matter or Occupy; you claim these have been violently repressed, but we shouldn't be naive.  They weren't/aren't accomplishing anything substantive and were/are more of a nuisance than transformative forces.  Millions of labor-hours spent standing in protest, but Bernie Sanders' campaign never had the cash to match Clinton or Trump and no national politician is going to stick their neck out for BLM because their political activity is so incompatible with electoral politics.  Republicans still hand Dems their asses in local elections because while Liberals protest, Republicans vote.  Is that they system failing, or the voters?

Because it seems like the people who ignore the "your vote doesn't matter" meme are kicking the shit out of the folks who buy it...by voting.

All these movements are (in my estimation) more identitarian than political. A group concerned about police policy/racism could back alternative candidates if they were a reliable voting constituency that could be spoken to by candidates.  They aren't, so we get Democrats trying to navigate a *problematic* minefield of "do I say black lives or all lives?!" (never mind what policies would have been) while Republicans speak a direct message that reflects policy and reassures voters.

>"Why don't you run for office then" is a pretty funny joke in the average American household, because the idea that a normal, common American could have a reasonable chance of winning is laughable.

That's false, and it reveals a serious misunderstanding of the kind of power we actually have.

I know a 27 year-old woman who ran for city council and defeated a well-funded incumbent because...Facebook.  I have a great deal of respect for her and mean this as no disrespect to her, but it's not like she's some mutant prodigy.  She's a fairly normal person who (near as I can tell) didn't like the way the previous councilwoman had been doing her job, and decided to oppose her while proposing concrete solutions to problems the community faced.  She won even though she had much less money.

There are fewer national-level elected politicians than there are starting players in the NFL.  It should be no surprise at all that a random person with no apparent qualifications has no chance of winning those posts, and our effective requirement that you be somehow notable and apparently qualified before you have a chance is a *feature*, not a failure.

So no, you shouldn't run for office.  You should volunteer for a campaign or develop an interest in local politics.  You might not have a chance in hell at being a Senator, but the school board isn't beyond the stars - and if you want to know why Republicans control so much of the government right now, it's because they took that lesson to heart.

They and their voters took it on faith that voting mattered, and now...
